label: Shoresh = Full Surface Word
description: Students treat the entire visible word as the shoresh instead of stripping non-root material or recognizing that a bare past form may already be too obvious.
likely_causes: Overgeneralizing from bare citation-like past forms
Insufficient segmentation practice before shoresh questions
suggested_reteach_moves: Show the same shoresh in one bare form and one affixed form
Ask which letters keep the meaning across both forms

label: Prefix Absorbed Into Shoresh
description: Students mistake a meaningful prefixed letter for part of the shoresh.
suggested_reteach_moves: Use split-word exercises with the prefix already isolated
Compare a base form and its prefixed future form

label: Suffix Absorbed Into Shoresh
description: Students leave possessive or subject endings inside the shoresh.
likely_causes: Insufficient contrast between root letters and endings
suggested_reteach_moves: Have the learner remove the ending and then say the remaining shoresh aloud
Color-code endings across several examples from the same family

label: Noun Suffix vs Verb Ending
description: Students confuse noun possession endings with verb subject or object endings.
likely_causes: Over-reading a familiar ending without checking part of speech
suggested_reteach_moves: Force a noun/verb choice before asking about the ending
Use matched pairs with the same final letters on a noun and a verb

label: Tav-Future Ambiguity
description: Students assume a prefixed ? has one fixed subject value instead of recognizing the she-will / you-will ambiguity.
likely_causes: Over-memorizing one translation for a marker
Not using context to settle subject identity
suggested_reteach_moves: Show the same form in two short phrases with different subjects
Ask what extra context would settle the reading

label: Misreading Vav Transformations
description: Students read every prefixed vav as simple and without noticing its interaction with tense.
likely_causes: Treating all vav-prefixed verbs as one pattern
suggested_reteach_moves: Contrast a ?? future-reading form with a ?? past-reading form side by side
Highlight the vowel difference before asking for translation

label: Past Plural vs Imperative Plural
description: Students confuse a ??-ending past plural form with a direct command to many men.
likely_causes: Insufficient exposure to imperative families in phrase context
suggested_reteach_moves: Use one token in two mini-sentences and compare the reading
Ask which English sentence sounds like a command

label: Subject Ending vs Object Suffix
description: Students treat attached object pronouns as if they were subject markers or vice versa.
likely_causes: Not separating who did from done to whom
suggested_reteach_moves: Annotate verb core, subject, and attached object as separate slots
Ask the learner to state both subject and object in English

label: False Plural Surface Reading
description: Students assume every ??-looking or similar plural-looking surface is an ordinary plural noun.
suggested_reteach_moves: Teach a short reviewed exception bank
Contrast a true plural with a false-plural-looking form

label: Literal Translation Over Context
description: Students keep the base gloss even when phrase or pasuk context requires a different English rendering or word order.
likely_causes: Not separating base meaning from here meaning
suggested_reteach_moves: Show base meaning and in-context meaning side by side
Ask why the literal gloss sounds wrong in the phrase

label: Present/Habitual Ignored
description: Students force a present/habitual form into a past or future bucket because those are more familiar.
likely_causes: Weak attention to pattern-based present forms
suggested_reteach_moves: Contrast one shoresh across past, future, and present/habitual forms
Use classification tasks before translation tasks

label: Nun Future vs Passive-Like Pattern
description: Students over-assign prefixed ? to we will or to passive-like meaning without enough evidence.
likely_causes: Weak differentiation between first-person plural and passive-like stem behavior
suggested_reteach_moves: Keep nun-prefixed forms in reviewed phrase context until stable
Explain why the same consonant marker can belong to different systems

label: Over-Reliance on First/Last-Letter Cues
description: Students answer from the first or last visible letter instead of identifying the full pattern family.
suggested_reteach_moves: Use contrastive sets that share first letters but differ in pattern
Require the learner to name the full marker family, not just one letter

label: Nikud-Sensitive Distinction Missed
description: Students ignore vowels that differentiate tense, pattern family, or derived-stem reading.
likely_causes: Weak comparison of near-identical forms with different vowels
suggested_reteach_moves: Show minimally different pointed forms side by side
Highlight the vowel that changes the reading before asking for translation
